mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the major saltwater body of the hydrosphere (A.1.a), physical and chemical properties of seawater including salinity, dissolved gases, and pH (B), ocean-atmosphere interactions influencing weather and climate (E), environmental threats such as pollution, acidification, and warming (F), ocean waves and tides (G), and ocean current systems both surface and deep (H).

Addresses saltwater bodies including marginal seas (A.1), seawater chemistry covering salinity, pH, and dissolved gases (B), ocean-atmosphere interactions and climate moderation (E), environmental issues including marine pollution (F), waves, tides, and tsunamis (G), and surface and deep thermohaline circulation (H).

Addresses the hydrosphere broadly, noting oceans cover 70.8% of Earth's surface (A), the role of greenhouse effect and weather systems in the water cycle (D and E), and Earth's origin and atmospheric evolution relevant to ocean-atmosphere interactions (E).

Directly covers freshwater lakes as enclosed water bodies on land (A.2.a), including limnology, thermal stratification, water chemistry, and the role of lakes in the hydrological cycle (C). Covers tectonic, volcanic, glacial, and artificial lake origins.

Covers the global distribution of water across oceans, freshwater bodies, and biological fluids (A), molecular structure and properties relevant to seawater and freshwater chemistry (B, C), the global water cycle including evaporation, precipitation, and groundwater (D), and phase transitions relevant to ice in the hydrosphere (A.3).

The largest oceanic division, directly relevant to oceans and marginal seas (A.1.a). Covers the Mariana Trench, seamount chains, Coriolis-driven circulation (H), and El Nino patterns relevant to ocean-atmosphere interactions.

The smallest oceanic division (A.1.a), characterized by sea ice dynamics including icebergs and pack ice (A.3.b), low salinity properties distinct from other oceans (B), and connections to Atlantic and Pacific via ocean current pathways (H).

Covers atmospheric water vapor and its role in the hydrologic cycle through evaporation and condensation (D.2, D.3), and ocean-atmosphere interactions including greenhouse effect, weather systems, and climate moderation (E).

An intercontinental marginal sea (A.1.a) with gulf and bay characteristics (A.1.b), covering unique oceanographic properties including the Messinian salinity crisis and circulation patterns relevant to seawater chemistry (B).

Rivers are freshwater channels flowing toward oceans and lakes (A.2.a), playing a central role in runoff and subsurface water within the hydrologic cycle (D.4), including drainage basins, discharge, and groundwater seepage.

Rising CO2 and ocean warming directly address the ocean as a carbon dioxide sink (E.2), impacts on ocean-atmosphere interactions and climate anomalies including Arctic amplification and sea level rise (E.3), and greenhouse warming disrupting the water balance (F).

Rain is the dominant precipitation mechanism, formed through atmospheric water vapor condensation (D.3), sustaining river systems and groundwater recharge as part of the hydrologic cycle, and shaping water resources and supply globally (D.6).

Tropical cyclones are sustained by heat energy from warm ocean surfaces, directly illustrating ocean-atmosphere interactions and climate anomalies (E.3). They generate storm surges and ocean waves (G.1).

CO2 dissolves in ocean water forming carbonic acid, causing ocean acidification, directly relevant to the ocean as a carbon dioxide sink (E.2). Rising atmospheric CO2 from fossil fuels drives greenhouse warming and potential water balance disruption (F).
Clouds form from atmospheric water vapor condensation (D.3) and modulate Earth's energy balance through radiation budgets relevant to ocean-atmosphere interactions (E.1).

Snowpack compacts into firn and glacial ice relevant to ice sheets and glaciers (A.3.a), and snow serves as a critical freshwater reservoir regulating seasonal runoff and groundwater recharge as part of the ice component of the hydrologic cycle (D.5).

Holds approximately 70% of global freshwater as ice sheets (A.3.a), directly relevant to ice in the hydrologic cycle and sea-level implications (D.5). Southern Ocean circulation connects to ocean current systems.

Floods represent extreme runoff events within the hydrologic cycle (D.4), driven by riverbank overflow, storm surge, and intensified water cycle from climate change, connecting to water resources management (D.6).
The third-largest oceanic division (A.1.a) with monsoon-driven climate and Walker circulation patterns, unique upwelling, and ocean current systems (H).
The largest freshwater lake system containing 21% of Earth's surface fresh water (A.2.a), with properties relevant to freshwater chemistry including ecology, invasive species, and water quality (C).

The world's largest inland body of water, an endorheic basin with salinity far below seawater (A.2.a), covering hydrology, freshwater properties, and environmental concerns including projected depth decline from climate change (C).
Ecology's study of organism-environment relationships connects to water in the biosphere (A.4), including aquatic ecosystems, energy flow, and nutrient cycling in hydrospheric contexts.

Wind drives ocean-atmosphere interactions through differential solar heating and the Coriolis effect (E.3), generates surface waves in the hydrosphere (G.1), and is a primary force causing ocean currents via pressure gradients and frictional forces (H.2).
Earthquakes generate tsunamis, a major type of surface wave in the hydrosphere (G.1), through sudden seismic energy release along tectonic plate boundaries.
The Amazon plays a disproportionate role in South American hydrology through rainfall feedback and carbon storage, connecting to the hydrologic cycle (D) and water in the biosphere (A.4).
Converts gravitational energy of flowing water into electricity, directly relevant to water resources and supply (D.6), including river basin management and the broader water-energy nexus.
Water pollution, acidification of precipitation from sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxide emissions, and other contaminants directly address human effects on the hydrosphere (F).
China's longest river draining one-fifth of its land area (A.2.a), with periodic flooding and the Three Gorges Dam illustrating runoff and water resource management within the hydrologic cycle (D.4).
A major Himalayan river forming one of the world's largest delta systems (A.2.a), with hydrology, irrigation, and dams relevant to runoff and subsurface water in the hydrologic cycle (D.4).

The Arctic region encompasses sea ice, permafrost, and seasonal ice dynamics including icebergs and pack ice (A.3.b), with accelerating sea-ice loss illustrating ocean-atmosphere interactions and climate anomalies (E.3).
Earth's 4.54-billion-year history includes oceanic and atmospheric formation, relevant to the origin and distribution of water in the hydrosphere (A).
Fossil fuel combustion releases greenhouse gases driving ocean acidification, relevant to the ocean as a CO2 sink (E.2), and disrupting the water balance through greenhouse warming (F).
